Core Data & Persistence.question: What is Core Data?
Core Data & Persistence.answer: Core Data is a framework that is used to manage model layer objects. It has the ability to persist object graphs to a persistent store. Data is organized into relational entity-attribute model.

Core Data & Persistence.question: What is a managed object context?
Core Data & Persistence.answer: First, managed object context is an instance of NSManagedObjectContext. It is the central object in the Core Data stack. It is used to create and fetch managed objects, and to manage undo and redo operations. Although it is allowed to have multiple managed object contexts, there is typically at most one managed object to represent any given record in a persistent store.

Core Data & Persistence.question: What is the difference between keychain and user defaults?
Core Data & Persistence.answer:
* A **keychain** is an encrypted container that holds passwords for multiple applications and secure services. Apple Inc. uses keychains as password management system in Mac OS and iOS.
* **NSUserDefaults** Provides a way for application behavior customization based on user preferences. Belongs to the Foundation framework for Cocoa and Cocoa Touch.
* Addition: When we saved userName and Password. and Remove app from device.
* In Keychain: UserName and Password still is there.
* In NSUserDefaults: UserName and Password also remove from device with your app.



Note that NSUserDefaults is not thread safe as it is a singleton object which is not thread safe

Core Data & Persistence.question: 1. Talk about CoreData.
Core Data & Persistence.answer:
CoreData is used as a framework for managing object graphs. It helps manage a graph of object instances that is potentially very large. This allows the app to use a graph that would not completely fit in the memory, by bringing objects in and out of the memory when needed. CoreData also helps in managing the limitations on properties and relationships and in maintaining the integrity of references. This means that when objects are added to or removed from a relationship, the forward and backward links remain consistent. Therefore, CoreData is considered to be the perfect framework for creating the “model” of an MVC architecture.

CoreData uses SQLite for disk storage to implement graph management. It is possible to implement graph management using some other relational database or even a non-relational database like CouchDB. CoreData is more of an API that abstracts from the actual datastore than a database engine. Therefore you can tell how CoreData must store itself - as a plist, an SQLite database, a binary file, or a custom data store type.

*Giving a detailed answer to an iOS interview question like this is good, but your answer can become great if you can add your perspective as well to the answer.*

Core Data & Persistence.question: Q: What is Keychain?
Core Data & Persistence.answer: Keychain is an API for persisting data securely in an iOS App.

Objective-C.question: When would you use Core Data over NSUserDefault?
Objective-C.answer: NSUserDefault is typically used to store small bits of data (settings, preferences, etc.). Core Data is used to store a large list of elements.

Objective-C.question: What is NSFetchRequest?
Objective-C.answer: NSFetchRequest is the class responsible for fetching from Core Data. Fetch requests can be used to fetch a set of objects meeting a certain criteria, individual values and more. ([source](https://medium.com/ios-os-x-development/50-ios-interview-questions-and-answers-part-2-45f952230b9f))

Objective-C.question: What considerations do you need when writing a UITableViewController which shows images downloaded from a remote server?
Objective-C.answer:
- Only download the image when the cell is scrolled into view (when cellForRowAtIndexPath is called)
- Download the image asynchronously on a background thread so as not to block the UI so the user can keep scrolling
- When the image has downloaded for a cell, check if that cell is still in the view or whether it has been re-used by another piece of data. If the cell has been re-used, then the image should be discarded. Otherwise, it should be switched back to the main thread to change the image on the cell. ([source](https://www.codementor.io/mattgoldspink/ios-interview-tips-questions-answers-objective-c-du1088nfb))

Objective-C.question: What is waterfall methodology and Agile methodology? What are the differences between them?
Objective-C.answer:
Waterfall methodology is a sequential model for software development. It is separated into a sequence of pre-defined phases including feasibility, planning, design, build, test, production, and support.

On the other hand, Agile development methodology is a linear sequential apporach that provides flexibility for changing project requirements.

List of differences:
- Waterfall model divides software development process into different phases while Agile segregates the project development lifecycle into sprints. This makes waterfall more rigid while agile allows for more flexibility
- Waterfall model describes the software development life cycle as a single project while Agile considers it as a collection of many different projects; are iterations of different phases focusing on improving overall software quality with feedback from users and QA team.
- Since waterfall is more rigid, development requirements need to be clearly established beforehand since there is little flexibility for changing once project development starts. Meanwhile, Agile allow changes to be made anytime along the project development process even after initial planning has been completed.
- In Waterfall, the testing phase typically occurs after the build phase. In Agile, testing is often performed concurrently with programming or at least in the same iteration.
- Waterfall is more of an interal process that does not involve user feedback. Agile tends to involve user participation more in order to improve customer satisfaction.
- Waterfall model best fits projects that have a clearly defined set of requirements and where change to requirements is not expect. Agile fits more for projects where the requirements are expected to change and evolve.
- Waterfall can exhibit a project mindset that focuses on completion of the project while Agile can allow for more focus on developing a product that satisfies customers.

Objective-C.question: What is JSON? What are the pros and cons?
Objective-C.answer:
JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation. According to [wiki](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON), it is a file format that uses human-readable text to transmite data objects consisting of attribute-value pairs and array data types. 

Pros:

- It is lighter than XML meaning that it can represent the same data in XML in fewer bytes. This makes network transmissions and read/writes faster
- Since it is native to JavaScript, computationally-expensive XSL tranformations are not needed in order to extract data

Cons:

- Not as widespread as XML
- Data is not readily streamable and has to be broken up into individual objects
- Can't use comments

Objective-C.question: Is it faster to search for an item in an NSArray or an NSSet?
Objective-C.answer: It depends. NSSet is faster to lookup an item in but can hold at most one of any given object. The reason is because NSSet uses hash values in order to find items. NSArray can hold multiple copies of an object but is slower to search for an item as it has to iterate through its entire contents to find it. ([source - #25](https://medium.com/cocoaacademymag/25-ios-interview-questions-and-answers-for-junior-developers-19bfe6e99b0))

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between *copy* and *retain*?
Objective-C.answer:
Calling *retain* on an object will increase its *retain* count by one. When the *retain* count of an objective reaches 0, the object will be deallocated and released from memory.

When you *retain* an object, you share the same version with whoever passed the object to you. But when you *copy* an object, you do not share the same version of the object that was passed to you. Instead, a duplicate of that object is created with duplicated values.

Objective-C.question: What is synthesize in Objective-C?
Objective-C.answer: *Synthesize* generates getter and setter methods for your property.

Objective-C.question: What is dynamic in Objective-C?
Objective-C.answer: Dynamic is used for subclasses of NSManagedObject. @dynamic can also be used to delegate the responsibility of implementing the accessors. ([source](https://medium.com/ios-os-x-development/ios-interview-questions-13840247a57a))

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between _ vs self. in Objective-C?
Objective-C.answer:
You typically use either when accessing a property in Objective-C. When you use _, you're referencing the actual instance variable directly. You should avoid this. Instead, you should use self. to ensure that any getter/setter actions are honored. 

In the case that you would write your own setter method, using _ would not call that setter method. Using self. on the property, however, would call the setter method you implemented.

Objective-C.question: What are blocks in Objective-C?
Objective-C.answer:
Blocks are a language-level feature of Objective (C and C++ too). They are objects that allow you to create distinct segments of code that can be passed around to methods or functions as if they were values. This means that a block is capable of being added to collections such as NSArray or NSDictionary. Blocks are also able to take arguments and return values similar to methods and functions.

The syntax to define a block literal uses the caret symbol(^):

<details open>
<summary>Objective-C</summary>
    
```objective-c
^{
  NSLog(@"This is an example of a block")
}
```
</details>

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between category and extension in Objective-C?
Objective-C.answer: A category and extension are similar in functionality where they can add additional instance and class methods to a class. However, an extension can only do so if the source code for the class being extended is available at compile time. This means that classes such as NSString cannot be extended. Instead, a category would be used to add additional methods to the NSString class

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between public and open? Why is it important to have both?
Objective-C.answer:
Open access imposes limitations on class inheritance. Classes declared with open level access can be subclassed by modules they are defined in, modules that import the module in which the class is defined, and class members as well. While this sounds similar to the public access level defined in Swift 2, there is a small difference. In Swift 3, the meaning of public access level means that classes declared public can only be subclassed in the module they are defined in. This includes public class members which can be overridden by subclasses defined int he module they are defined in.

Some classes of libraries and frameworks are not designed to be subclasses. For example, in the Core Data framework, Apple states that some methods of NSManagedObject should not be overridden. To prevent any unexpected behavior that may result from overriding those methods, Apple declares those methods public rather than open. As a result, those methods are not marked as open for developers to override. ([source](https://cocoacasts.com/what-is-the-difference-between-public-and-open-in-swift-3/))

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between *atomic* and *non-atomic* synthesized properties?
Objective-C.answer:
First, properties are set to *atomic* by default. 

*Atomic* properties are more likely to guarentee thread-safety because it will ensure that a value is fully set (by the setter method) or fully retrieved (by the getter method) when accessor methods are called simultaneously.

*Non-atomic* properties, however are not thread-safe. While they run faster, they may cause race conditions. In the event that accessor methods are called simultaneously and a race condition occurs, a setter value would first release the old value and a getter method would retrieve nil since no value has not been set yet.

Objective-C.question: Spot the bug that occurs in the code:
Objective-C.answer:
Swift ```swift class ViewController: UIViewController { @IBOutlet var alert: UILabel! override func viewDidLoad() { super.viewDidLoad() let frame: CGRect = CGRect(x: 100, y: 100, width: 100, height: 50) self.alert = UILabel(frame: frame) self.alert.text = "Please wait..." self.view.addSubview(self.alert) } DispatchQueue.global(qos: .default).async { sleep(10) self.alert.text = "Waiting over" } } ```
Objective-C ```objective-c @interface MyCustomController : UIViewController @property (strong, nonatomic) UILabel *alert; @end @implementation MyCustomController - (void)viewDidLoad { CGRect frame = CGRectMake(100, 100, 100, 50); self.alert = [[UILabel alloc] initWithFrame:frame]; self.alert.text = @"Please wait..."; [self.view addSubview:self.alert]; dispatch_async( dispatch_get_global_queue(DISPATCH_QUEUE_PRIORITY_DEFAULT, 0), ^{ sleep(10); self.alert.text = @"Waiting over"; } ); } ```

All UI updates must be performed on the main thread. Global dispatch queues do not make any guarantees so code should be modified to run the UI update on the main thread. Here is the fix below:
Swift ```swift DispatchQueue.global(qos: .default).async { sleep(10) DispatchQueue.main.async { self.alert.text = "Waiting over" } } ```
Objective-C ``` dispatch_async( dispatch_get_global_queue(DISPATCH_QUEUE_PRIORITY_DEFAULT, 0), ^{ sleep(10); dispatch_async(dispatch_get_main_queue(), ^{ self.alert.text = @"Waiting over"; }); }); ```

Objective-C.question: What is the purpose of unit/UI testing? What are the benefits?
Objective-C.answer: Unit/UI testing are the basic of test-driven development. This development approach allows you to codify requirements for your code before you implement it. Unit tests are important to make sure that code meets its design and requirements and behaves as expected. Parts of the program are segregated and tested to ensure that individual parts are working correctly.

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between viewDidLoad and viewDidAppear? Which should you use to load data from a remote server to display in the view?
Objective-C.answer:
viewDidLoad is only called when the view is loaded (after loadView is called). viewDidAppear, on the other hand, is called everytime the view appears on the device. 

If the data is static for the most part, it can be loaded in viewDidLoad and cached. But if the data is dynamic and likely to change often, it is preferrable to use viewDidAppear. In both instances, data should be loaded asynchronously on a background thread to avoid blocking the UI.

Objective-C.question: What is the reuseIdentifier for?
Objective-C.answer: The *reuseIdentifier* indicates that cells for a UITableView (or UICollectionView) can be reused. UITableView maintains an internal cache of UITableViewCell with the appropriate identifier and allows them to be reused when dequeueForCellWithReuseIdentifier is called. As a result, this increases performance of UITableView since a new view does not have to be created for a cell.

Objective-C.question: What is Auto Layout?
Objective-C.answer: Auto Layout is used to dynamically calculate the size and position of views based on constraints.

Objective-C.question: There's a typo / an incorrect answer to one of your questions
Objective-C.answer: Great! Creating an issue will let me know what changes should be made. You can even making the changes yourself and make a pull request which will expedite the process!

Objective-C.question: Requesting questions to be answered and topics
Objective-C.answer: If you open an issue, I would be happy to go ahead and add the question with the appropriate answer when I get to it!

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between composition vs aggregation vs association?
What are the 3 main types of design patterns?
Objective-C.answer:
1- Creational : Singleton, Factory
2- Behavioral : Observer
3- Structural : Decorator, Adapter, Facade

Objective-C.question: What are the main design principles?
Objective-C.answer:
**Recap**

| Low-level Design Principles | High-level Design Principles |
| --- | --- |
| 1) Tell don’t Ask | 1) Single Responsibility Principle |
| 2) Once and Only Once | 2) Open Closed Principle |
| 3) Law of Demeter | 3) Liskov Substitution Principle |
| 4) Favor Composition over Inheritance | 4) Interface Segregation Principle |
| 5) Command Query Separation | 5) Dependency Inversion Principle |

**1- Tell don’t ask:**
This means instructing a method to perform the calculation and return the final result directly, rather than fetching the variables and performing the calculation externally where the method was called.

**2- Once and only once:**
This implies avoiding code duplication. If a block of code is repeated multiple times, it should be extracted and encapsulated within its own dedicated method.

**3- Law of Demeter:**
This dictates that an object should only communicate with its immediate neighbors (objects it holds a direct reference to). It should not chain method calls through multiple objects to retrieve data (e.g., Object A calling Object B to get Object C, to get Object D). The correct approach is to communicate with the immediate dependency, which then communicates with its own dependency, continuing this chain until the data is retrieved and returned in the same manner.

**4- Favour composition over inheritance:**
This is preferred because composition allows for behavior changes dynamically at runtime, whereas inheritance establishes a rigid, static structure that cannot be altered during execution.

**5- Query command separation:**
Methods should either make calculations or retrieve data, but one method shouldn’t do both.

Objective-C.question: What are the downsides of using Singleton design pattern?
Objective-C.answer:
1-It’s not thread safe, if we didn’t take care of the initialization we might end up having two or more objects.
2- Hard in testing.
3- Hard to mock.

Objective-C.question: Value and Reference Types
Objective-C.answer: Types in Swift fall into one of two categories: first, "value types", where each instance keeps a unique copy of its data, usually defined as a `struct`, `enum`, or tuple. The second, "reference types", where instances share a single copy of the data, and the type is usually defined as a `class`. In this post we explore the merits of value and reference types, and how to choose between them.

Objective-C.question: What is the difference between Designated and Convenience initializers?
Objective-C.answer:
Designated initializer is the default initializer (only one designated per class)
Convenience initializer is the second , third, fourth … constructor you want to implement, need convenience keyword before init.
So if you want many constructor you have to make one as designated (just init() ), the others (convenience init(params) )

Objective-C.question: Constraints that prevent me from making KVO on property?
Can we capture a lazy variable inside a closure or not?
How do you handle deadlock situations in iOS ?
What are higher order functions ?
Objective-C.answer: Higher order functions are simply functions that operate on other functions by either taking a function as an argument, or returning a function. Swift’s Array type has a few methods that are higher order functions: sorted, map, filter, and reduce. These methods use closures to allow us to pass in functionality that can then determine how we want the method to sort, map, filter, or reduce an array of objects.

Objective-C.question: What is Quality Of Service (QoS)?
What is intrinsic content size?
Objective-C.answer:
Intrinsic content size is information that a view has about how big it should be based on what it displays. For example, a label’s intrinsic content size is based on how much text it is displaying. In your case, the uiimageview intrinsic content size is the size of the image that you selected.
Auto Layout takes this information into consideration by creating intrinsic content size constraints for each view. Unlike other constraints, these constraints have two priorities: a content hugging priority and a content compression resistance priority.

Objective-C.question: What is Regression?
Objective-C.answer: Regression occurs when a modification is made to the code base, but the expected output remains the same. If the previously passing test cases subsequently fail after this change, it indicates that the core functionality has been unintentionally altered or broken.

Objective-C.question: 2. What is the difference between copy and retain?
Objective-C.answer:
Generally, retaining an object increases its retain count by one. This helps retain the object in memory instead of being blown away. Therefore, if you only have the retained version of an object, you will share that copy of the same with whoever sent it to you.

Copying an object, on the other hand, creates an extra copy or a duplicate version of the object. While you share the original with the person who sent the object to you, you do not share the copied or duplicate version with the sender.

Objective-C.question: 8. Mention some design patterns apart from the common Cocoa patterns.
Objective-C.answer:
Apart from MVC, Singleton, Observer, and Delegate patterns that are commonly used, there are other patterns too that could be used for developing iOS applications:
* **Factory Method:** With the Factory Method, one can replace class constructors for abstracting and hiding initialization of objects so that the type is determined when the application is run. This method also helps to hide and contain the switch/if statements that help identify which objects to create instances for.
* **Adapter:** With the help of the Adapter design pattern one can adapt one object’s interface to that of the other. It is useful when a third-party code cannot be changed to one’s own code or when something with an inconvenient or incompatible API needs to be used.
* **Decorator:** The Decorator is a wrapper that can be wrapped around different classes to enhance their capabilities. One can wrap it around things one wants to decorate. Once wrapped, it implements the interface and delegates the messages that were sent to it to the underlying objects, or it changes and improves objects or gives its own implementation to them.
* **Command:** The Command design pattern allows you to implement an object representing an operation that you want to perform. The operation you want to execute may have its own state and logic for performing the task. As an advantage, you can hide the operation’s internal implementation from the users, add undo/redo capabilities to it, and even execute operations later instead of at the time of operation creation.
* **Template:** The Template design pattern has a base class outlining the algorithm for what is required to be done. There are several abstract methods of the base class. These abstract methods must be implemented by the concrete subclasses of the base class. These abstract methods are also referred to as hook methods. The interaction of the Template Method class users happens through the base class that implements the algorithm steps. The subclasses supply the concrete implementations for those steps.

Objective-C.question: 9. Mention different options for implementing storage and persistence in iOS.
Objective-C.answer:
Outlined below are the ways to store data in simple to complex order:
* For intermediate data storage or for storage that doesn’t need to last long, we can use in-memory arrays, dictionaries, sets, and other data structures.
* For simple key-value storage, we can use NSUserDefaults/Keychain. Here, one thing is insecure, and the other is secure, respectively.
* For writing data to or from a disk, we can take the help of NSFileManager, whether the data is serialized or not, we can use File or Disk storage.
* When trying to simplify working with databases, frameworks like Core Data and Realm are useful.
* When your requirements include implementing complex querying mechanics, the SQLite relational database is what you should use.

*The above iOS interview question is an example of questions where you can use the traditional answer outline above and be safe, but if you add examples of having implemented each or any of the options above, you may earn the interviewer’s attention.*

Objective-C.question: 12. Explain overlays.
Objective-C.answer:
Overlays are a technique used in operating systems to allow programs to use more memory than they would otherwise be able to.
When a program is loaded into memory, it takes up a certain amount of space. If the program is large and requires more memory than is available, overlays can be used to load only the portions of the program that are currently needed into memory. As the program executes, different portions can be swapped in and out of memory as needed.

Objective-C.question: 15. Explain ButtonStyle protocol in Swift.
Objective-C.answer: ButtonStyle protocol allow to customize new button styles that can be reused without the need for new views. Using this protocol, you can create a button that will appear the way you want. You will make your own style that sets a decent background for a button. You can use the `buttonStyle(_:)` modifier.

Objective-C.question: 23. Through what observable objects announce modifications to SwiftUI?
Objective-C.answer:
Using two primary ways `@Published` property wrapper or calling `objectWillChange.send()`:
* **@Published property wrapper:** It is very useful in SwiftUI. It allows us to create observable objects that automatically announce when changes occur.
* **objectWillChange:** It is a property defined on the ObservableObject protocol. Whenever any `@Published` properties of the objects change, the compiler synthesizes a default implementation for the property, which emits a value.

Objective-C.question: 25. Can we add a stored property to type through an extension?
Objective-C.answer:
No, we cannot add a stored property to a type through an extension in Swift.
Extensions in Swift can add computed properties, instance methods, class methods, and initializers to a type, but they cannot add stored properties. This is because stored properties must be initialized when an instance of the type is created, and extensions cannot provide their own initializers.

Objective-C.question: 28. What are reference types?
Objective-C.answer: A reference type contains the location in the memory where data lives. A reference type is passed to a function; instead of creating a new copy of the object, it just creates another reference to that object which is shared among all the different references.

Objective-C.question: 30. What is Regular Expression?
Objective-C.answer: They are special string patterns that tell how to search for a string. It is a sequence of characters that specifies a search pattern in the text; it finds or finds and replaces operations on strings.

Objective-C.question: 33. Explain processor management.
Objective-C.answer: It provides tools and resources to steer business processes toward better performance. It is an execution unit in which a program operates. It ensures that each process and application receives enough of the processor’s time to function properly and deallocates after the process is completed.

Objective-C.question: 34. Name different types of control transfer statements used in Swift.
Objective-C.answer:
* Continue
* Fallthrough
* Return
* Break
* Throw

Objective-C.question: 3. What is NSUserDefaults?
Objective-C.answer: `NSUserDefaults` provides the simplest method to store small amounts of data without requiring a database, utilizing a key-value pair system. It offers a programmatic interface for interacting with the default system settings. Through this default system, an application can modify its behavior to match a user's preferences (for example, saving a user's preferred media playback speed so it persists across sessions).

Objective-C.question: 4. Which types are supported by NSUserDefaults?
Objective-C.answer:
The primary data types supported by `NSUserDefaults` are:
* `NSString`
* `NSDate`
* `NSDictionary`
* `NSData`
* `NSNumber`

Objective-C.question: 5. What is the use of reuseIdentifier?
Objective-C.answer: The `reuseIdentifier` is used to group together visually similar rows in a `UITableView` (i.e., rows that differ only in their content but share the same layout). A `UITableView` object maintains a queue of currently reusable cells, each labeled with its own reuse identifier, making them accessible to the delegate via the `dequeueReusableCell` method. This drastically improves scrolling performance and memory management.

Objective-C.question: 6. Tell one difference between viewDidLoad and viewDidAppear.
Objective-C.answer:
* `viewDidLoad` is called exactly once when the view controller successfully loads its view hierarchy into memory. At this stage, you can instantiate variables and configure views that will remain throughout the lifecycle of this view controller.
* `viewDidAppear` is called every time the view transitions onto the device's screen. At this stage, you can perform layout actions, trigger animations, or execute any dynamic drawing in the User Interface.

Objective-C.question: 14. Explain one difference between atomic and nonatomic properties.
Objective-C.answer:
* **Atomic properties** (the default behavior in Objective-C) are thread-safe, meaning that only one thread can access the variable at a time. However, this safety lock makes them slower.
* **Nonatomic properties** are not thread-safe, meaning multiple threads can access the variable simultaneously (dynamic type). This makes them significantly faster, but it requires the developer to explicitly add the `nonatomic` keyword. If two threads access a nonatomic variable simultaneously, it can lead to unexpected behavior.

Objective-C.question: 20. Which API is suitable for writing test scripts to interact with the application’s UI elements?
Objective-C.answer: The **UI Automation API** is highly suitable, as it simulates user interaction with the application's interface and successfully returns detailed log information regarding the tests.

Objective-C.question: 22. What is the difference between an 'App ID' and a 'Bundle ID'? Why are they used?
Objective-C.answer:
* **App ID:** This is a unique identifier generated by Apple to identify a specific application developed by a specific registered development team. It is utilized to enable secure backend services like push notifications, set up app groups, and configure the app for the App Store.
* **Bundle ID:** This is an identifier specified by the developer within Xcode that uniquely identifies the application locally. It is used by the operating system to identify the app's executable, map its underlying resources, and manage its metadata.

Objective-C.question: 23. Tell one difference between Cocoa and Cocoa Touch.
Objective-C.answer: **Cocoa** is the application framework used for building desktop applications for macOS, whereas **Cocoa Touch** is the framework specifically optimized for building touch-based applications that run on iOS (iPhones and iPads).

Objective-C.question: 27. What is Objective-C in iOS?
Objective-C.answer: Objective-C is an object-oriented programming language previously utilized as Apple's primary language for OS development. It combines the syntax and flow control benefits of C with the object-oriented messaging capabilities of Smalltalk. It acts as a superset of C, maintaining C's primitive types while adding specific syntax for defining classes and methods.

Objective-C.question: 28. List the important data types in Objective-C.
Objective-C.answer:
* `BOOL`
* `NSInteger`
* `NSUInteger`
* `NSString`

Objective-C.question: 29. What is the use of NotificationCenter?
Objective-C.answer: `NotificationCenter` is a framework feature in iOS and macOS used for broadcasting and receiving system or custom messages across different parts of an application. It allows disparate objects to communicate without tightly coupling them together. *(Note: The provided text also mentions "Notification centers" in the context of the OS-level UI that displays notification history to the user, though programmatically, `NotificationCenter` acts as the internal broadcast hub).*

Objective-C.question: 31. What are design patterns in iOS?
Objective-C.answer:
Design patterns are proven, repeatable solutions to commonly occurring software engineering problems that arise during an application's architectural design. They serve as templates designed to make code more robust, scalable, and easier to maintain. Common design patterns include:

* Facade
* Decorator
* Memento
* Adapter

Objective-C.question: 33. What are iBeacons in iOS?
Objective-C.answer: *(Content ends here based on the provided text)*

Objective-C.question: 3. What is Test-Driven Development (TDD)?
Objective-C.answer: Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a software development methodology where test cases are written to define the desired software characteristics *before* the actual code is written. It provides valuable insights into both code quality and architectural design. Developers create a test for a specific functionality; when the test fails, they write the minimum amount of straightforward, bug-free code required to pass that test, subsequently refactoring as needed.

Objective-C.question: 6. What are the different smart groups in Xcode?
Objective-C.answer:
* **Simple Smart Group:** A basic organizational group that allows you to filter your project files by type, name, or path.
* **Simple Expression Smart Group:** This group enables filtering using more complex logical expressions, such as "name contains 'ViewController' AND path contains 'Controllers'."
* **Custom Smart Group:** This offers the highest level of flexibility, allowing you to create customized filters using advanced predicates.

Objective-C.question: 10. What is the use of design patterns in Linux?
Objective-C.answer: *(Note: While Linux is an OS, design patterns universally apply to software engineering.)* Design patterns are utilized to solve recurring problems in software architecture. They assist in writing simplified, structured code by providing proven templates. By applying design patterns, developers express a conceptual solution rather than a strict implementation, resulting in software that is highly flexible, reusable, and easier to maintain.

Objective-C.question: 11. List some common Cocoa Design patterns.
Objective-C.answer:
* **Creational - Singleton:** Ensures that a class has only one globally accessible instance. It is highly useful for sharing resources or configuration settings across an entire application.
* **Behavioral - Memento:** Allows an object's internal state to be captured, saved externally, and restored later without violating encapsulation. It is frequently used for Undo/Redo functionality.
* **Structural - Decorator:** Enables behaviors to be added to individual objects dynamically without altering the class structure. This is ideal for modifying object behavior at runtime or stacking functional wrappers.

Objective-C.question: 14. List the APIs for battery-efficient location tracking.
Objective-C.answer:
* **Significant-Change Location Service:** Highly power-efficient; it delivers updates only when the user’s location changes significantly (e.g., transitioning between cell towers, often tracking movements up to 1km).
* **Region Monitoring (Geofencing):** Triggers enter or exit events when the device crosses a defined geographical boundary (typically a radius of 100 meters or more).
* **Visit Events:** Monitors and logs when a user arrives at or departs from frequently visited locations (like a home or office).

Objective-C.question: 18. What is the use of computed properties?
Objective-C.answer: Computed properties do not physically store a value in memory. Instead, they provide a getter (and optionally a setter) to calculate and return a value dynamically based on underlying logic or the state of other stored properties every time they are accessed.

Objective-C.question: 19. What is dynamic dispatch?
Objective-C.answer: Dynamic dispatch is the runtime mechanism that determines which specific implementation of a polymorphic method or function to invoke. It provides architectural flexibility by allowing subclasses to override methods inherited from a parent class, ensuring the correct subclass implementation is executed when called.

Objective-C.question: 20. What are the different kinds of operations possible on a semaphore?
Objective-C.answer:
A semaphore relies on two primary atomic operations to manage thread synchronization:

* **Wait():** Decrements the semaphore value by 1. If the value is already 0, the thread attempting the wait operation is blocked until the value becomes greater than 0.
* **Signal():** Increments the semaphore value by 1. If other threads were blocked waiting on this semaphore, one of those threads is unblocked and permitted to proceed.

Objective-C.question: 21. Define RTOS.
Objective-C.answer: **RTOS** stands for Real-Time Operating System. It is engineered specifically for applications subjected to strict time constraints, where data processing must occur flawlessly within rigid deadlines (often measured in tenths of seconds). It is heavily used in systems interacting dynamically with external hardware events.

Objective-C.question: 23. List two types of process synchronization.
Objective-C.answer:
* **Interprocess Communication (IPC):** Mechanisms that allow distinct processes to communicate and synchronize their state (e.g., shared memory, message passing).
* **Mutual Exclusion (Mutex):** Techniques designed to ensure that only a single process or thread can access a shared resource or critical section of code at any given time (e.g., locks, binary semaphores).

Objective-C.question: 26. Can you describe how an app transitions when launched?
Objective-C.answer: When launched, an application transitions from the **Not Running** state into the **Inactive** state (running in the foreground but not yet receiving user events). Very shortly after, it transitions into the **Active** state, where it fully runs and responds to interactions. If dismissed by the user, it moves to the **Background** state, and eventually into the **Suspended** state.

Objective-C.question: 29. When will you use deinit?
Objective-C.answer: You utilize `deinit` to perform manual memory cleanup operations immediately before a class instance is permanently deallocated. While Swift's ARC handles standard memory deallocation automatically, `deinit` is vital for releasing custom system resources, invalidating timers, or removing notification observers.

Objective-C.question: 30. What methods will you use to reduce the size of the App?
Objective-C.answer:
* **App Thinning:** A comprehensive suite of techniques to optimize installation size based on the user's specific device.
* **App Slicing:** A subset of App Thinning where the App Store delivers only the assets (like 2x vs 3x images) strictly required for the target device's architecture.
* **On-Demand Resources (ODR):** Hosting heavy, non-immediate assets (like later game levels) on Apple's servers to be downloaded only when the user needs them.
* **Bitcode:** Compiling an intermediate representation of the code so Apple can re-optimize the binary on their end. *(Note: Apple deprecated Bitcode in Xcode 14, but it remains historically relevant).*
* **Asset Cleanup:** Routinely identifying and removing obsolete or unused images and files from the project bundle.

Objective-C.question: 31. From two choices—viewDidLoad and viewDidAppear—which will you choose to load data from a remote server to display in the view?
Objective-C.answer:
The choice strictly depends on the data's lifecycle requirements:

* Use **`viewDidLoad`** if the remote data is entirely static, highly cacheable, and only needs to be fetched exactly once when the view hierarchy is first created in memory.
* Use **`viewDidAppear`** if the data is highly dynamic or changes frequently, as this method is called every time the view is presented to the user, ensuring the content is always fresh.

Objective-C.question: 33. What is the difference between a synchronous and asynchronous NSURLSession in iOS?
Objective-C.answer:
* A **Synchronous** network request forcibly blocks the thread it is called on until the entire data transfer completes. Doing this on the main thread causes the UI to freeze entirely.
* An **Asynchronous** request dispatches the operation to a background thread, allowing the main thread to continue processing user interactions unhindered. A completion handler or delegate is notified once the download finishes.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain the difference between points and pixels?
Objective-C.answer: A **pixel** on iOS is the full resolution of the device, meaning if I have an image that is 100x100 pixels in length, the phone will render it 100x100 pixels on a standard non-retina device. However, because newer iPhones have a quadrupled pixel density, that same image will render at 100x100 pixels but look half that size. A **point** is a standard length equivalent to 1x1 pixels on a non-retina device, and 2x2 pixels on a retina device.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain view transitions?
Objective-C.answer: View Transitions are effective ways of adding one view on another view with a proper transition animation effect.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain the Update Cycle?
Objective-C.answer:
When an iOS app launches, `UIApplication` starts the main run loop for an app which runs on the main thread. The main run loop processes events (such as user touches) and handles updates to view-based interfaces. As events occur (such as touch, location updates, motion, and multimedia control), the run loop finds the appropriate handler for the events, calling appropriate methods, which call other methods, and so on. At some moment in time, all events will be handled, and control will return to the run loop. This point where control is returned to the run loop is called the **update cycle**.

While the events are being processed and some changes are requested to the view, these changes are not updated immediately. Instead, the system waits for the existing process to finish and when the next redraw cycle is going to happen. There is a periodic interval between event processing and UI layout update handling.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain setNeedsLayout vs layoutIfNeeded vs layoutSubviews?
Objective-C.answer:
* **setNeedsLayout:** Tells the system that you want it to layout and redraw a view and all of its subviews *when it is time for the next update cycle*. This is an asynchronous activity. It allows you to consolidate all layout updates into one cycle, which is usually better for performance.
* **layoutIfNeeded:** A synchronous call that tells the system you want a layout and redraw of a view and its subviews *done immediately* without waiting for the update cycle.
* **layoutSubviews:** The default implementation uses constraints to determine the size and position of subviews. Subclasses can override this method to perform more precise layouts. You should not call this method directly.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain the 3 phases of layouts?
Objective-C.answer:
There are three phases associated with the layout and drawing of views:
1. **Constraint pass:** Update of constraints (happens bottom-up).
2. **Layout pass:** Layout of views and subviews (happens top-down and is dependent on constraint settings).
3. **Display pass:** Where the views get redrawn based on the layout pass.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is intrinsic content size?
Objective-C.answer: With Auto Layout, a view’s content plays an important role in its layout. This is expressed through its `intrinsicContentSize`, which describes the minimum space needed to express the full view content without squeezing or clipping that data.

Objective-C.question: Q: What are layer objects?
Objective-C.answer: Layer objects are data objects that represent visual content and are used by views to render their content. Custom layer objects can be added to implement complex animations and sophisticated visual effects.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Auto resizing mask?
Objective-C.answer: An integer bit mask that determines how the receiver resizes itself when its superview’s bounds change. You specify the value by combining constants in `UIViewAutoresizing`. The default value is `none`.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Auto Layout?
Objective-C.answer: Auto Layout dynamically calculates the size and position of all views in your view hierarchy based on constraints placed on those views. This constraint-based approach allows you to build user interfaces that dynamically respond to internal and external changes.

Objective-C.question: Q: fileprivate VS private VS internal access level?
Objective-C.answer:
* **Internal:** Entities can be used within any source file from their defining module.
* **File-private:** Restricts the use of an entity strictly to its own defining source file.
* **Private:** Restricts the use of an entity to the enclosing declaration and to extensions of that declaration that are in the same file.

Objective-C.question: Q: Extensions in Swift?
Objective-C.answer: Extensions add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type. They can add computed properties, define methods, provide new initializers, define subscripts, and make existing types conform to protocols.

Objective-C.question: Q: map vs flatMap?
Objective-C.answer:
* **map:** Loops over a collection and applies a transform function to each item, returning an array of results.
* **flatMap:** Iterates, transforms, and flattens a collection of collections. It also removes optionals and `nil` values from the resulting array.

Objective-C.question: Q: filter VS reduce?
Objective-C.answer:
* **filter:** Loops over a collection and returns an array containing only elements that match a specific condition.
* **reduce:** Combines all items in a collection to create a single new aggregated value.

Objective-C.question: Q: Type safety & Type Inference?
Objective-C.answer:
* **Type Safety:** Swift ensures you are clear about the types of values your code works with. You cannot pass an `Int` to a `String` variable.
* **Type Inference:** If you don’t specify the type, the compiler automatically deduces the type based on the provided initial value.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is Downcasting?
Objective-C.answer:
* `as`: Upcasting and bridging.
* `as?`: Safe downcasting; returns `nil` if it fails.
* `as!`: Forced downcasting; crashes if it fails.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is Nil Coalescing & the Ternary Operator?
Objective-C.answer:
* **Ternary Operator:** `question ? answer1 : answer2` (evaluates a condition to return one of two expressions).
* **Nil-Coalescing:** `a ?? b` (unwraps optional `a` if it has a value, otherwise returns default value `b`).

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Stored Property?
Objective-C.answer: Stored properties use `var` (variables) or `let` (constants). If you create an instance of a value type (struct) and assign it to a `let` constant, you cannot modify its properties, even if they were declared as variables. This is not true for classes (reference types).

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Type Property?
Objective-C.answer: Properties that belong to the type itself, not an instance. Defined with the `static` keyword. For class types, `class` can be used instead of `static` to allow subclasses to override them.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Two Phase Initialization?
Objective-C.answer:
1. **Phase 1:** Each stored property is assigned an initial value by the class that introduced it.
2. **Phase 2:** Each class is given the opportunity to further customize its stored properties before the instance is ready.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Failable initializers?
Objective-C.answer: Denoted by `init?`, this initializer creates an *optional* instance that can fail. Use `return nil` within the initializer to trigger a failure.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Enum and associated value?
Objective-C.answer:
An enumeration consists of a set of related values. Associated values allow you to attach specific variables/types to individual enum cases.

```swift
enum Barcode {
  case upc(Int, Int, Int, Int)
  case qrCode(String)
}

```

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain Implicitly Assigned Raw Values?
Objective-C.answer: When assigning integer or string raw values to an enum, you don't have to define every case. Swift automatically assigns them (e.g., integers automatically increment by 1 starting from 0).

Objective-C.question: Q: Define @synchronized?
Objective-C.answer: The `@synchronized` directive is a convenient way to create mutex locks on the fly in Objective-C code. The `@synchronized` directive does what any other mutex lock would do—it prevents different threads from acquiring the same lock at the same time.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is NSBundle and main bundle?
Objective-C.answer:
A bundle is a structure used for packaging software on Mac OS X. Applications, frameworks, and plug-ins are all different kinds of bundles. Bundles may contain executable code, resources, header files, and other stuff (including other bundles) if you so wish. 

Bundles are implemented as directory trees with a defined structure. Applications, frameworks, and plug-ins each have their own variation on the structure of the tree. However, to the Finder, bundles look like single files. The main bundle is simply the bundle of the application that is running. So, for instance, the main bundle of the Apple mail program is `/Applications/Mail.app`.

Objective-C.question: Q: Define ‘id’ type in Objective-C?
Objective-C.answer: `id` is a data type of object identifiers in Objective-C, which can be used for an object of any type no matter what class it has, except native or primitive types. `id` is the final supertype of all objects.

Objective-C.question: Q: Define NSUserDefaults?
Objective-C.answer:
With the `NSUserDefaults` class, you can save settings and properties related to application or user data. For example, you could save a profile image set by the user or a default color scheme for the application. The objects will be saved in what is known as the iOS “defaults system”. The iOS defaults system is available throughout all of the code in your app, and any data saved to the defaults system will persist through application sessions. This means that even if the user closes your application or reboots their phone, the saved data will still be available the next time they open the app!

With `NSUserDefaults` you can save objects from the following class types: `NSData`, `NSString`, `NSNumber`, `NSDate`, `NSArray`, `NSDictionary`. If you want to store any other type of object, such as a `UIImage`, you will typically need to archive it or wrap it in an instance of `NSData`, `NSNumber`, or `NSString`.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is the APNS payload size?
Objective-C.answer:
As per the updated Apple docs, the sizes are:
* For regular remote notifications, the maximum size is **4KB** (4096 bytes).
* For Voice over Internet Protocol (VoIP) notifications, the maximum size is **5KB** (5120 bytes).
* *Note:* If you are using the legacy APNs binary interface to send notifications instead of an HTTP/2 request, the maximum payload size is **2KB** (2048 bytes).

Objective-C.question: Q: Define Quality of Service (QoS)?
Objective-C.answer:
The quality of service API was introduced in iOS 8. QoS can be applied all over iOS. One can prioritize queues, thread objects, dispatch queues, and POSIX threads. This is important since asynchronous work is typically spread out across all of these techniques. By assigning the correct priority, iOS apps remain quick, snappy, and responsive.

* **User Interactive:** Work that happens on the main thread, such as animations or drawing operations.
* **User Initiated:** Work that the user kicks off and should yield immediate results. This work must be completed for the user to continue.
* **Utility:** Work that may take a bit and doesn’t need to finish right away. Analogous to progress bars and importing data.
* **Background:** This work isn’t visible to the user. Backups, syncs, indexing, etc.
* **Default:** The priority level falls between user-initiated and utility. Work with no QoS information assigned is treated as default, and the GCD global queue runs at this level.
* **Unspecified:** Represents the absence of QoS information and cues the system that an environmental QoS should be inferred.

Objective-C.question: Q: What are VoIP notifications?
Objective-C.answer:
A few of the advantages are:
* The app is automatically relaunched if it’s not running when a VoIP push is received.
* The device is woken up only when a VoIP push occurs (saves battery).
* VoIP pushes go straight to your app for processing and are delivered without delay.

Apple provides us with a framework called `PushKit` to support using this VoIP push feature. We also need to generate a VoIP push certificate for implementation.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is Dynamic Dispatch?
Objective-C.answer: Dynamic Dispatch is the process of selecting which implementation of a polymorphic operation (a method or a function) to call at run time. This means that when we want to invoke our methods (like an object method), the system determines the correct implementation to execute dynamically.

Objective-C.question: Q: Why do we need to specify `self` to refer to a stored property or a method when writing asynchronous code?
Objective-C.answer: Since the code is dispatched to a background thread, we need to carefully capture a reference to the correct object to ensure it remains in memory and we access the correct instance.

Objective-C.question: Q: What are the biggest changes in UserNotifications?
Objective-C.answer:
* We can add audio, video, and images.
* We can create custom interfaces for notifications.
* We can manage notifications with interfaces in the notification center.
* New Notification extensions allow us to manage remote notification payloads before they’re delivered.

Objective-C.question: Q: Define Autorelease pool?
Q: What is Method Swizzling?
Objective-C.answer: Swizzling is the act of changing the functionality of a method by replacing the implementation of that method with another, usually at runtime. Reasons to use swizzling include: introspection, overriding default behavior, or dynamic method loading. This dynamic program modification is similar to monkey patching.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is NSCoding?
Objective-C.answer: `NSCoding` is a protocol that enables an object to be encoded and decoded for archiving and distribution (e.g., saving custom objects to `UserDefaults` or a file).

Objective-C.question: Q: What is fast enumeration?
Q: Difference between ‘+’ and ‘-’ notation in methods?
Objective-C.answer: In Objective-C, **Instance methods** begin with `-` and **Class level methods** begin with `+`.

Objective-C.question: Q: Class vs Instance vs Static methods
Objective-C.answer:
* **Instance methods:** Functions that belong to instances of a particular class, structure, or enumeration. They provide ways to access and modify instance properties.
* **Class methods:** Called on the type itself (type methods). Indicated by writing the keyword `class` before the method’s `func` keyword. Subclasses can override `class` methods since they are dynamically dispatched.
* **Static methods:** Indicated by writing the keyword `static`. Subclasses *cannot* override `static` methods. `static` can be considered essentially as a "Class final" method.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is NSXMLParserDelegate?
Objective-C.answer: A protocol in the Foundation framework used to define the behavior of a delegate object that manages the parsing of XML documents using `XMLParser`.

Objective-C.question: Q: Which APIs are used for battery-efficient location tracking?
Objective-C.answer:
* **Significant location changes:** The location is delivered approximately every 500 meters (usually up to 1 km).
* **Region monitoring:** Tracks enter/exit events from geographical regions with a radius equal to 100m or more.
* **Visit events:** Monitors enter/exit events from frequently visited places (like a home or office).

Objective-C.question: Q: What is Continuous Integration?
Objective-C.answer: Continuous Integration (CI) is a development practice that requires developers to integrate code into a shared repository several times a day. Each check-in is then verified by an automated build, allowing teams to detect problems early (e.g., Xcode server, Jenkins, Travis, Fastlane).

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain UNNotification Content?
Objective-C.answer: `UNNotificationContent` stores the content data inside a scheduled or delivered notification. It is read-only.

Objective-C.question: Q: Explain In-app Purchase products and subscriptions?
Objective-C.answer:
* **Consumable products:** Can be purchased more than once (used items would have to be re-purchased).
* **Non-consumable products:** Users can restore this functionality in the future if they reinstall the app.
* **Non-Renewing Subscription:** Used for a certain amount of time and certain content.
* **Auto-Renewing Subscription:** Used for recurring monthly subscriptions.

Objective-C.question: Q: What kind of benefits does Xcode server have for developers?
Objective-C.answer: Xcode server automatically checks out a project from source control, builds the app, runs automated tests, and archives the app for distribution.

Objective-C.question: Q: What are layer objects and what do they represent?
Objective-C.answer: Layer objects (`CALayer`) represent visual content and are used by views to actually render their content to the screen. They can be heavily manipulated to implement complex animations and sophisticated visual effects without the overhead of heavy views.

Objective-C.question: Q: @synthesize VS @dynamic
Objective-C.answer:
* **`@synthesize`** will generate getter and setter methods for your property at compile time.
* **`@dynamic`** just tells the compiler that the getter and setter methods are implemented not by the class itself but somewhere else (like the superclass or will be provided at runtime). Uses for `@dynamic` are e.g., with subclasses of `NSManagedObject` (CoreData) or when you want to create an outlet for a property defined by a superclass that was not defined as an outlet.

Objective-C.question: Q: NSInteger VS Int VS NSNumber
Objective-C.answer:
* **`NSInteger`** is a type definition that describes an integer — but it is NOT equivalent to `int` on 64-bit platforms. It is defined as `int` when building a 32-bit app and as `long` for 64-bit apps. Most of the time you can replace `int` with `NSInteger`, but there are some things to consider when doing so.
* **`Int`** is a primitive data type in Swift.
* **`NSNumber`** stores numeric types as objects and can convert them into different formats. It can also retrieve a string representation.

Objective-C.question: Q: Any VS AnyObject
Objective-C.answer:
According to Apple’s Swift documentation:
* **`Any`** can represent an instance of any type at all, including function types and optional types.
* **`AnyObject`** can represent an instance of any class type.

Objective-C.question: Q: Category VS Protocol
Objective-C.answer:
*This comparison is usually for the Objective-C implementation where we have the concept of categories. If we need to convert the respective functionality into Swift then we can use protocols with optional and required keywords.*
* **Protocol** declares a set of methods that a class must implement. Methods can be marked as optional or required for the implementing class by `@optional` and `@required` keywords.
* **Category** adds methods to an existing class (e.g., `NSObject`), without modifying the class itself.

Objective-C.question: Q: Category VS Inheritance
Objective-C.answer:
* **Categories** allow expanding the API of existing classes without changing their type. It is used to add new methods, not properties.
* **Inheritance** also expands the API but introduces a new type. Additionally, subclassing lets you add state and add properties as well. *(Note: We can add properties in categories through Associated Objects).*

Objective-C.question: Q: Category VS Extension
Objective-C.answer:
*(Note: Extensions in Swift are similar to categories in Objective-C. Unlike Objective-C categories, Swift extensions do not have names.)*
* **Extensions (Swift)** add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type. This includes the ability to extend types for which you do not have access to the original source code.
* **Categories (Objective-C only)** allow you to add methods outside of the main interface file. 
* **Extensions (Objective-C)** must be implemented in the main Interface file. This means we cannot use Objective-C extensions for extending Built-in classes or Classes for which you don’t have the source code; there you should use Categories.

Objective-C.question: Q: NSSet VS NSArray
Objective-C.answer:
* **`NSSet`** primarily accesses items by comparison. They are unordered, and no duplication is allowed.
* **`NSArray`** accesses items by index. They allow duplicate items and are ordered.

Objective-C.question: Q: NSDictionary VS NSMutableArray
Objective-C.answer:
* **`NSDictionary`** is unordered and stores objects as key-value pairs. Objects are accessed by addressing them with an arbitrary string value.
* **`NSMutableArray`** is similar to `NSArray` except that its content is dynamic (i.e., elements can be added/removed/changed after creating an object).

Objective-C.question: Q: Delegate VS Notifications
Objective-C.answer:
* **Delegates** create a "has-a" relationship between two classes. It supports two-way communication (i.e., we can return a value and it also has a chance to modify or reject operations). It checks protocol method implementation at compile time. Only one designated object can listen to the message. Similar to a telephonic conversation. Delegation also avoids tight coupling and modifies behavior and appearance without the need to subclass objects.
* **Notifications** are based on one-to-many communication. They cannot receive feedback and have no direct link between the objects communicating back and forth. There is no compile-time checking for method implementation. Any number of objects can receive the message.

Objective-C.question: Q: Shallow VS Deep Copy
Objective-C.answer:
* **Shallow Copy** is also known as address copy, similar to Call by Reference. It copies the structure, not the data/element. Changes made in one object reflect in the other one as well. In Objective-C, it can be implemented with the `copy` or `mutableCopy` keyword (for collections, this copies the collection structure but not the elements).
* **Deep Copy** is similar to Call by Value. It copies actual data and both objects have their own separate copy (i.e., they point to a different array and different elements). Changes made in one object do not reflect in another one. In Objective-C, it can be implemented using methods of the `NSKeyedArchiver`/`NSKeyedUnarchiver` classes.

Objective-C.question: Q: Atomic VS NonAtomic
Objective-C.answer:
* **Atomic** is the default behavior for properties. It ensures the present process is completed by the CPU before another process accesses the variable, hence it is thread-safe. It is not fast, as it ensures the process is completed entirely (adds a lock).
* **Non-Atomic** is not the default behavior. It is faster (for synthesized code, that is, for variables created using `@property` and `@synthesize`). It is not thread-safe and may result in unexpected behavior when two different threads access the same variable at the same time.
*(Note: A piece of code is called thread-safe if it gives correct results even in a multi-threaded environment.)*

Objective-C.question: Q: Retain VS Copy
Objective-C.answer:
* **`retain`**: It is done on the created object, and it just increases the reference count by one.
* **`copy`**: Makes a full copy of an object, and returns it with a retain count of 1. If you copy an object, you own the copy. This applies to any method that contains the word `copy` where “copy” refers to the object being returned.

Objective-C.question: Q: Nil VS nil VS NSNull
Objective-C.answer:
* **`nil`** (Objective-C): A literal null value for Objective-C objects (e.g., `id obj = nil;`).
* **`Nil`** (Objective-C): A literal null value for Objective-C classes (e.g., `Class cls = Nil;`).
* **`NSNull`**: A singleton object used to represent a null value in collection objects (like `NSArray` or `NSDictionary`) which do not allow `nil` values.

Objective-C.question: Q: NSNotificationCenter VS local notification VS push notification
Objective-C.answer:
* **UILocalNotification:** Represents notifications that an application can schedule for presentation to its users at specific dates and times. The OS delivers the notification. It does not notify custom class objects. It appears as an alert if the app is in the background. If the app is running, `UIApplicationDelegate`'s `didReceiveLocalNotification` method gets called.
* **NSNotificationCenter:** Internal to the running app and doesn’t require a server or special setup. It is used to notify custom class objects internally. `NSNotification` is a message sent internally between objects in the app.
* **Push Notification:** Requires Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles in the Apple Developer portal. This requires a server and special setup. The message is sent from the server through APNs to the device, and the app handles displaying it.

Objective-C.question: Q: Key-Value Observing VS NSNotificationCenter
Objective-C.answer:
* **Key-Value Observing (KVO)** adds observers for a specific *keypath* and is used to observe changes in a property of a single object.
* **NSNotificationCenter** adds observers for *notifications* and is used when multiple, decoupled objects need to be notified of an event.

Objective-C.question: Q: Content Hugging VS Compression Resistance
Objective-C.answer:
* **Intrinsic Content Size:** Information a view has about how big it should be based on what it displays (e.g., a `UILabel`'s size based on its text, or a `UIImageView`'s size based on the image). 
* **Content Hugging (Content does not want to grow):** The higher this priority is, the more a view resists growing larger than its intrinsic content size.
* **Compression Resistance (Content does not want to shrink):** The higher this priority is, the more a view resists shrinking smaller than its intrinsic content size.

Objective-C.question: Q: GCD VS NSOperation
Objective-C.answer:
* **GCD** is a low-level C-based API.
* **NSOperation** and **NSOperationQueue** are Objective-C classes (an Objective-C wrapper over GCD).

**GCD advantages over NSOperation:**
* **Implementation:** Simple, lightweight, and uses lock-free algorithms. `NSOperationQueue` is complex, heavyweight, and involves locking.

**NSOperation advantages over GCD:**
* **Control:** You can Pause, Cancel, and Resume an `NSOperation`.
* **Dependencies:** You can set up dependencies between two `NSOperations` (an operation will not start until its dependencies finish).
* **State of Operation:** You can monitor the state (`ready`, `executing`, `finished`) and compute pending operations.
* **Concurrency Limits:** You can specify the maximum number of queued operations that can run simultaneously.
* **Observable:** The properties of `NSOperation` can be observed using KVO.

**When to use which:** Use `NSOperation` when you want more control over the queue, states, and dependencies. Use GCD for simple cases where you want less overhead to quickly dispatch work to the background.

Objective-C.question: Q: Static Vs Dynamic typing
Objective-C.answer:
* **Static typing** is when type checking occurs at compile time. You must define a type for variables in your code, and operations are checked by the compiler.
* **Dynamic typing** is when type checking occurs at runtime. You will get runtime errors if trying to perform operations on incompatible types, but it provides the benefit of writing versatile functions that handle multiple data types.

Objective-C.question: Q: What is an “app ID” and a “bundle ID”?
Objective-C.answer:
* **Bundle ID:** A unique identifier of an app in Apple’s ecosystem. No two apps can have the same bundle ID. It lets macOS/iOS recognize updates to your app and is used in validating the application signature.
* **App ID:** A two-part string used to identify one or more apps from a single development team. It consists of the Team ID (supplied by Apple) and the bundle ID search string. There are explicit App IDs (for a single app) and wildcard App IDs (for a set of apps).

Objective-C.question: Q: REST VS SOAP
Objective-C.answer:
* **REST (Representational State Transfer):** An architectural style utilizing standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE). Uses lightweight formats like JSON.
* **SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol):** A strict protocol that relies exclusively on XML for messaging and often requires more bandwidth and overhead.

Objective-C.question: Q: Static VS Dynamic Library
Objective-C.answer:
* **Static Library (.a):** Allows an application to load code into its address space at *compile time*. Results in a larger app size on disk and slower build times. Code is directly linked into the binary, meaning updates to the library require recompiling the entire app.
* **Dynamic Library (.dylib / .framework):** Loads code into the address space at *run time*. Results in smaller binaries and allows libraries to be updated independently without recompiling the executable. They can have their own initializers and cleanup tasks.

Objective-C.question: Q: CollectionViews VS TableViews
Objective-C.answer:
* **TableViews:** Display a list of items in a single column and are limited to vertical scrolling only.
* **CollectionViews:** Display items in highly customizable layouts (multiple columns, grids, rows) and can be scrolled vertically and horizontally.

Objective-C.question: Q: LLVM VS Clang
Objective-C.answer:
* **Clang:** The front end of the LLVM toolchain for the C language family (C, C++, Objective-C). It takes the source code, performs lexical analysis and parsing, and generates an abstract syntax tree (LLVM IR).
* **LLVM:** The broader compiler infrastructure. It takes the intermediate representation (IR) from Clang, optimizes it, and serves as the backend to generate machine code for the target architecture.

Objective-C.question: Introduction
Objective-C.answer:
Reactive programming is used to handle mutable data processing and threads, such as fetching large amounts of data from an API. It addresses several key areas:

1. **State:** Specifically, managing shared mutable state (the state of the data).
2. **Imperative programming:** Traditional programming uses functions to tell the app exactly *when* and *how* to do things, which can lead to complex state management.
3. **Side effects:** Issues arising during error handling or abnormal data processing (any change of state outside the current scope).
4. **Declarative code:** Here, we specify functions that define pieces of behavior, and Rx executes this code. This eliminates issues related to side effects and state changes commonly found in imperative programming.
5. **Reactive systems:** This is crucial for determining how the user interacts and how the response behaves. Reactive systems are:
    * **Responsive:** Always keep the UI up to date, representing the latest app state.
    * **Resilient:** Each behavior is defined in isolation, providing flexible error recovery.
    * **Elastic:** The code handles varied workloads, often implementing features such as lazy pull-driven data collections, event throttling, and resource sharing.
    * **Message-driven:** Components use message-based communication for improved reusability and isolation, decoupling the lifecycle and implementation of classes.

Objective-C.question: The Three Building Blocks
Objective-C.answer: So, how does this happen? It happens through the three building blocks of Rx code: **Observables**, **Operators**, and **Schedulers**.

Objective-C.question: 3. Schedulers
Objective-C.answer:
With all these events and subscriptions, there must be a mechanism to organize the processes. Therefore, Rx provides its own dispatchers called Schedulers. RxSwift acts as a dispatcher between your subscriptions (the work being done) and the schedulers (the execution context), sending pieces of work to the correct context and seamlessly allowing them to work with each other’s output.

---

Objective-C.question: The difference between composition vs aggregation vs association:
Objective-C.answer:
* **Composition** implies a relationship where the child cannot exist independent of the parent. 
    * *Example:* House (parent) and Room (child). Rooms don't exist separate to a House.
* **Aggregation** implies a relationship where the child can exist independently of the parent. 
    * *Example:* Class (parent) and Student (child). Delete the Class and the Students still exist.
* **Association** is a "relates-to" relationship between two objects, where one object is related to another object. In other words, the two objects have a relationship that is not based on composition or aggregation. 
    * *For example,* a student may take a course, and the student and the course are related to each other, but neither is a part of the other.

---

Objective-C.question: The difference between function overriding and overloading:
Objective-C.answer:
* **Function overloading** is a way to define multiple functions with the same name in a class or a module, but with different parameters. Overloading allows a function to behave differently based on the number, type, and order of its parameters. When a function is overloaded, the compiler determines which version of the function to call based on the arguments passed in at runtime.
* **Function overriding** is a way to provide a new implementation of a method that is already defined in a parent class or an interface. Overriding allows a subclass to provide its own implementation of a method that is inherited from its superclass. When a method is overridden, the version of the method that is called depends on the type of the object at runtime.

---

Objective-C.question: The difference between class-level properties, instance-level properties and static properties:
Objective-C.answer:
* **Class-level properties** are associated with a class and are shared among all instances of the class. They can be overridden by subclasses.
* **Instance-level properties** are associated with an instance of a class or a struct. Each instance has its own copy of the property.
* **Static properties** are associated with a type and are shared among all instances of the type. They cannot be overridden by subclasses.

---

Objective-C.question: 16- In an AppDelegate, how is the main UIWindow instantiated?
Objective-C.answer:
If you choose the Storyboard option as you specify a template, the process works a little differently. The app is given a main storyboard, pointed to by the Info.plist key “Main storyboard file base name” (UIMainStoryboardFile).
After UIApplicationMain instantiates the app delegate class, it asks the app delegate for the value of its window property; if that value is nil, the window is created and assigned to the app delegate’s window property. The storyboard’s initial view controller is then instantiated and assigned to the window’s rootViewController property, with the result that its view is placed in the window as its root view; the window is then sent the makeKeyAndVisible message. All of that is done behind the scenes by UIApplicationMain, with no visible code whatever. That is why, in a storyboard template, the application:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions: implementation is empty.
